retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Ensuring proper equipment is essential to a successful vaginal delivery and to minimize fetal and maternal morbidity and mortality. Appropriate equipment is necessary to anticipate and appropriately manage improbable but realistic complications of low-risk vaginal deliveries, as 20% to 25% of all perinatal morbidity and mortality occurs in pregnancies devoid of risk factors for adverse outcomes. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The list of equipment needed also includes a tocodynamometer to monitor uterine contractions using an external monitor or an intrauterine pressure catheter and fetal heart rate monitor with either external heart rate monitor or an internal fetal heart rate monitor (scalp electrode). If the delivery needs assistance, either forceps or vacuum can be kept bedside to assist in vaginal delivery. [22] Analgesia can be kept bedside, but is not absolutely needed, as the use of oral or epidural analgesia is based on maternal preference.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After the placenta is delivered, the vaginal canal should be inspected for any lacerations, and if lacerations are detected, they should be repaired.

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: A successful operative vaginal delivery requires a well-trained and experienced obstetrician. It may necessitate hundreds of deliveries with forceps to prevent or manage any maternal or fetal complications. A  pediatrician should also be present at the time of delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Cesarean Delivery -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For a fetus in vertex presentation, delivery involves inserting a hand into the uterine cavity to elevate the fetal head into the hysterotomy. If the elevation of the head is challenging, an assistant may assist by providing additional elevation from below via a hand in the patient's vagina. Alternatively, a vacuum cup or a single forceps blade may be used to elevate the fetal head. Once the fetal head is elevated into the incision, the bladder blade is removed, and fundal pressure is applied to expel the fetus from the uterus. The surgeon carefully guides the fetal head during delivery, while the surgical assistant often provides most of the fundal pressure. If fundal pressure is insufficient or difficult to achieve—such as in cases of significant maternal obesity—a vacuum cup can be applied to the fetal head to assist with delivery. Forceps may also be used during cesarean delivery while adhering to the same principles when using vacuum or forceps, even in the context of a cesarean section.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal delivery is safest for the fetus and the mother when the newborn is full-term at the gestational age of 37 to 42 weeks. Vaginal delivery is preferred considering the morbidity and the mortality associated with operative cesarean births has increased over time. [1] Approximately 80% of all singleton vaginal deliveries are at full-term via spontaneous labor, whereas 11% are preterm, and 10% are post-term. [2] Of note, with the advent of operative delivery modalities and surgical delivery modalities, the number of patients who reach spontaneous labor has decreased over time, and the induction of labor has increased. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the typical course of a patient undergoing vaginal delivery. Identify the most common adverse events associated with vaginal delivery. Summarize the risks associated with vaginal delivery. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients undergoing vaginal delivery. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The next step is to ensure the preferred forceps are readily available and in working order. Consider having a back-up instrument ready.  The use of vacuum extraction after failed forceps delivery is not recommended due to a higher rate of fetal complications. [11] Additionally, because perineal and vaginal lacerations are a common complication of forceps delivery, instruments and sutures for laceration repair should also be set up on the obstetric operative table.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: As with any procedure, appropriate preparation and positioning of the patient are key to maximizing the success of the procedure while simultaneously minimizing morbidity and mortality. There are many factors to consider in preparing a patient for a vaginal delivery and the position of the patient changes based on the progression of labor through its various stages.

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to continue with operative vaginal delivery should continuously be re-evaluated during the delivery progresses. If there is no descent, further attempts should be discontinued and cesarean section considered. Rocking motions and applying torque to achieve rotation should be avoided.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriate preparation includes a warm and clean room with adequate lighting and supplemental light source, a delivery bed with clean linen whose height can be adjusted, a plastic sheet to place under the mother, chlorhexidine, and wipes. There should be equipment for barrier protection such as gloves and masks. Sterile equipment includes appropriate sterile gloves, sterile instruments such as scissors, needle holders, artery forceps for cord clamping, dissecting forceps, sponge forceps, sterile blade, and cord ties. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Delivery -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal delivery is safest for the fetus and for the mother when the newborn is full-term at the gestational age of 37 to 42 weeks. Vaginal delivery is preferred considering the morbidity and the mortality associated with operative cesarean births has increased over time. This activity outlines the process of vaginal delivery, the technique, and preparation required to successfully accomplish a vaginal delivery, and reviews the role of an interprofessional team in managing labor and proving care for patients who undergo vaginal delivery as a means of childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A trained, experienced provider should perform operative vaginal delivery with forceps.

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Operative delivery using forceps requires an interprofessional approach where medical professionals from different departments work together to achieve a successful vaginal delivery and the safe delivery of a healthy infant. This approach requires planning in order to coordinate readiness from all the departments involved. Institutions who practice the use of forceps deliveries must have systems in place that allow the smooth transition from a planned event during a complicated delivery to an emergent intervention yielding a positive result in either scenario.

retrieved_docs.title: Cesarean Delivery -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The availability of a standardized surgical pack and cesarean instrument tray can be highly beneficial, particularly in emergency situations, as it reduces the time spent gathering the necessary equipment. In addition to the standard instrument trays for cesarean delivery, a hysterectomy instrument tray should also be readily available. While a peripartum hysterectomy is relatively rare, its incidence is increasing. Having the appropriate instruments on hand can help save valuable time in emergency situations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures is a broad category of obstetric interventions that encompasses specific techniques such as بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery, each classified as a child procedure within this group. Both بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery represent assisted vaginal delivery methods used to facilitate safe childbirth when spontaneous vaginal delivery is not optimal, highlighting the importance of tailored procedural options in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1790
retrieved_docs.content: Routine obstetric care, which includes comprehensive antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care, is broadly related to assisted delivery techniques such as forceps, vacuum extraction, and breech delivery, highlighting the integration of standard obstetric management with these specialized delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad obstetric category that encompasses the specific outcome of فشل التوليد بالملقط (forceps delivery failure). Within this framework, الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is directly linked to فشل التوليد بالملقط, indicating that the failure represents a particular adverse event or subcategory of the forceps delivery procedure. Consequently, both الولادة بالملقط and its associated failure, فشل التوليد بالملقط, are situated under the larger grouping of other procedures that induce or assist delivery, highlighting their interrelated roles in assisted vaginal birth and the importance of recognizing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures constitute a broad category of obstetric interventions, and Vaginal Assisted Delivery is identified as a child procedure within this category, indicating that Vaginal Assisted Delivery is a specific type of pregnancy-related procedure used to assist vaginal birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” includes a range of obstetric interventions used to facilitate childbirth, and within this group the specific condition “فشل التوليد بالملقط” (failure of delivery with forceps) is classified as a child concept. This relationship indicates that forceps‑related delivery failure is considered a particular outcome or complication associated with the broader set of delivery‑inducing or assisting procedures. Recognizing “فشل التوليد بالملقط” as a subcategory of “Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery” helps clinicians organize risk assessment, anticipate potential complications, and apply appropriate management strategies for assisted vaginal births.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery of vertex presentation refers to a vaginal delivery of the fetus when the baby is positioned head‑first, representing a specific type of vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2516
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery constitute a broad category of obstetric interventions that are employed to facilitate vaginal birth. Within this category, the specific complication فشل التوليد بالملقط (failure of forceps delivery) is identified as a child procedure, indicating that it is a particular adverse outcome associated with assisted delivery techniques. Episiotomy, a surgical incision made in the perineum to enlarge the vaginal opening, functions as a parent procedure within the same group, linking various delivery‑assisting methods and serving as a foundational technique for related interventions. Preventive incision, exemplified by episiotomy, is recognized as having a direct relationship to these obstetric procedures, underscoring the role of episiotomy as a preventive measure designed to reduce trauma during delivery and to support the successful execution of other assisted delivery methods. Together, these concepts illustrate how episiotomy and preventive incisions are integral components of the spectrum of procedures used to assist delivery, while also highlighting specific complications such as فشل التوليد بالملقط that can arise from assisted vaginal birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 809
retrieved_docs.content: Vacuum dilatation of cervix is a specific medical operation used to facilitate delivery that belongs to the broader category of “Operation to facilitate delivery.” It is classified as a “procedure categorized by device involved,” reflecting that the technique relies on a vacuum device to dilate the cervix during childbirth. Because procedures categorized by device involvement are themselves a subset of “إجراء تدخلي” (interventional procedures), vacuum dilatation of cervix is an interventional procedure that utilizes specialized equipment to achieve its therapeutic goal. This hierarchical relationship highlights how the technique fits within both device‑based procedural classifications and the general domain of interventions aimed at assisting vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: Bevalling door middel van vacuümextractie, a method of assisted vaginal delivery, corresponds to the Arabic term الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين, indicating that both refer to delivery by vacuum extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
retrieved_docs.content: Labor and delivery (الولادة) is integrated with multiple data sources, including generic “Databases” and the “OBSTETRICAL GYNECOLOGICAL DATA BASE.” Routine obstetric care—ante‑partum monitoring, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy, forceps, vacuum or breech assistance), and post‑partum follow‑up—is directly linked to delivery modalities such as cesarean delivery (الولادة القيصرية), vacuum extraction (الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين), and the composite category “forceps; vacuum; and breech delivery.” Cesarean delivery is a method of الولادة that includes specific actions (Extraction), incision types (low transverse cesarean section), and scar classifications (CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN). Clinical indications and contraindications surround labor: premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) is a parent condition precipitating labor; abnormal labor (مخاض غير طبيعي) requires specialized management; placenta previa (بضع الارتفاق) dictates operative techniques such as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction is a procedure that can be performed using the method الولادة القيصرية (cesarean delivery). Within the spectrum of extraction techniques, الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (vacuum‑assisted delivery) represents a narrower, more specific form of extraction. Routine obstetric care—including antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care—has a direct relationship with الولادة القيصرية, indicating that cesarean delivery is one of the delivery options considered in standard obstetric management. The same routine obstetric care also has a broader relationship with الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين, reflecting that vacuum‑assisted delivery is encompassed within the comprehensive range of services provided in routine obstetric care. Together, these connections illustrate how different delivery methods, from cesarean sections to vacuum extraction, are integrated into both specific extraction procedures and the overall framework of routine obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1123
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical trachelotomy is classified as an “Other procedures to assist delivery” and, within this framework, is considered synonymous with vaginal cesarean section. It is broadly linked to routine obstetric care—including antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care—and is associated with the concept of a preventive incision. Induction of labor by artificial rupture of membranes is identified as a child procedure under the category “Other procedures to assist delivery.” The category “Other procedures associated with delivery” functions as a parent of “Other procedures to assist delivery” and is also connected to repair of episiotomy, indicating that it encompasses interventions such as episiotomy repair. Together, these relationships illustrate how various delivery‑assisting interventions—obstetrical trachelotomy, vaginal cesarean section, artificial membrane rupture, and episiotomy repair—are organized within the broader context of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1769
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetric therapeutic procedures (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد) encompass a range of interventions used to manage labor and delivery, including episiotomy, artificial membrane rupture (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), and vacuum extraction (شفط بالتخلية). Episiotomy is a surgical incision of the perineum performed to enlarge the vaginal opening and facilitate fetal passage. Artificial membrane rupture involves the intentional breaking of the amniotic sac to accelerate labor progression. Vacuum extraction employs a suction device to assist the delivery of the fetus when vaginal birth requires additional traction. Together, these procedures represent key components of obstetric therapeutic care, providing clinicians with options to address various labor complications and to promote safe outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1997
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad category that includes the specific adverse outcome فشل التوليد بالملقط (failure of forceps delivery). The procedure الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is classified as a type of Vaginal Assisted Delivery, indicating that it is an assisted vaginal birth technique. Within this framework, فشل التوليد بالملقط is understood as a possible complication or subcategory of الولادة بالملقط, and both are situated under the larger group of other procedures that induce or assist delivery. This hierarchical organization clarifies that forceps delivery belongs to the family of assisted vaginal deliveries and that its potential failure is recognized as a distinct entity within obstetric practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2271
retrieved_docs.content: Enkelvoudige bevalling met behulp van forceps of vacuŸmextractor is a specific type of assisted vaginal birth that is classified under the broader group of forceps, vacuum, and breech delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery are classified as a subset of other procedures to assist delivery, indicating that these specific interventions are encompassed within the broader category of techniques used to facilitate and support the childbirth process.
